2002-12-01.title: U.S. and Philippines May Start New Training Mission
2002-12-01.abstract: US military officials say Americans and Philippines may soon start new military training operation against Muslim extremists in southern Philippines; it would involve 300 to 400 American troops, including many on jungle combat patrols in risky hunt for Abu Sayyaf guerrilla force; group was target of training mission carried out by American forces on island of Basilan earlier in year, but only one leader, Abu Sayyaf, was killed in that mission, and group's other leaders have regrouped in Sulu Province, principally on Jolo Island; photo; map (M)

2002-12-01.title: A Friend in India to All the World
2002-12-01.abstract: Amber miners of Simojovel, Mexico, earn very little for their backbreaking and dangerous work: salary of no more than $2 or $3 a day, plus as little as $2.50 a week for amber that they might hack from earth; they work hand-hewn, hundred-foot tunnels without benefit of safety standards, rules and regulations; Simojovel has one of world's few known mother lodes of amber, and its amber is reputed to be best; photos; map (M)

2002-12-01.title: Indonesian Human Rights Court Acquits 4 in East Timor Killings
2002-12-01.abstract: A P J Abdul Kalam, India's Muslim president, pays visit to Sri Sathya Sai Baba, Hindu holy man at ashram in Puttaparthy, who preaches unusual mixture of faiths and encourages nonviolence; photo; map (M)

2002-12-01.title: Saudi Arabia Is Called Slow in Helping Stem the Flow of Cash to Militants
2002-12-01.abstract: American officials find it hard to stop flow of money from wealthy Saudis and Saudi-financed charities to people and groups deemed by US to be supporting terrorist attacks around the world; report sponsored by Council on Foreign Relations in November said Saudi Arabia was largest source of financing for Al Qaeda and blamed both American and Saudi governments for not being tough enough; some 300 Saudi charities dispense as much as $4 billion a year (M)

2002-12-01.title: After Attack, Kenya Traces Qaeda's Trail in East Africa
2002-12-01.abstract: Investigators probing attacks on Israeli targets in Mombasa, Kenya, study evidence that East Africa has served as both useful base and target for Al Qaeda terror network for nearly decade; photo; Kenyan official Julius Sunkuli, cordinating probe with US and Israel, says Qaeda cannot be ruled out, although police have few leads into bombing of hotel and attempted missile attack on Israeli airliner; hotel attack killed ten Kenyans and 3 Israelis; police release American woman and her Spanish husband who apparently were trying to leave city because they were frightened; 6 Pakistanis and 4 Somalis are being questioned; Qaeda operations are especially difficult to track through Africa's porous borders (M)

2002-12-02.title: Bali's Broken Economy: As Fragile as an Eggshell
2002-12-02.abstract: Bali's tourism-based economy has collapsed in wake of Oct terrorist attack on disco; return of foreign visitors is only starting again; economists do not expect early recovery; say quick progress in arresting attack suspects could spur foreign governments to drop travel warnings; photos (M)

2002-12-02.title: BIZTECH: Hutchison's uphill struggle for 3G glory
2002-12-02.abstract: HONG KONG—  While most major Asian technology companies evolved from a skilled manufacturing base, Hong Kong's largest conglomerate, Hutchison Whampoa Ltd., has taken a telecom road less traveled.

2002-12-02.title: Athens Journal; The Priests in Black Yearn for Unorthodox Attire
2002-12-02.abstract: Orthodox priests in Greece are pressing for change in priestly dress code; some want permission to shed outer robe when not performing formal church service; others want permission to wear casual garb on streets; some even want beard requirement eased; many Greek Orthodox priests outside Greece dress much like Roman Catholic priests, with no complaints from superiors; photo (M)

2002-12-02.title: Britain Accuses Hussein of Systematic Torture and Killing
2002-12-02.abstract: Britain released a dossier today on what it called the systematic rape, torture and executions of Iraqis by Saddam Hussein.

2002-12-02.title: U.N. Team Gets to Work, Wary of Both Iraq and U.S.
2002-12-02.abstract: United Nations weapons inspectors and Iraqi officials, with only 13 of 1,000 suspected weapons sites checked so far, have already set pattern of tension and intrigue that is barely covered over with polite humor and vows of mutual interest; both inspectors and Iraqis have entered process knowing that war would be likely if inspections flounder or uncover new pattern of Iraqi deceit, and that generation of iron-fisted rule under Pres Saddam Hussein is hanging in balance; inspectors' grinding task is burdened by knowledge that hawks in Bush administration have scant faith in their ability to strip Iraq of its weapons of mass destruction after evasion and intransigence with which Iraqis met earlier generation of UN inspectors in 1990's; some in current UN inspection team suspect that Washington hard-liners might prefer them to fail, to clear path to military showdown; inspectors are caught between Americans and Iraqis, each pushing in different direction, as they set about task of huge technical and logistical complexity; photos (L)

2002-12-03.title: Offering an Incentive to Disarm
2002-12-03.abstract: United States Amb to Japan Howard H Baker Jr says Washington could respond with surprising speed to engage with North Korea if that country takes verifiable steps to dismantle its nuclear weapons program; says Pres Bush has made clear that he does not want conflict with North Korea (S)

2002-12-03.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Swedish Diplomats Expelled
2002-12-03.abstract: Russia expels two Swedish diplomats in reprisal for earlier expulsion of Russian diplomats from Stockholm; controversy stems from claim that Russians were participating in industrial espionage while in Sweden (S)

2002-12-03.title: C.I.A. Hunts Iraq Tie to Soviet Smallpox
2002-12-03.abstract: CIA investigates informant's accusation that Iraq obtained particularly virulent strain of smallpox from Russian scientist; American officials say several American scientists were told in August that Iraq might have obtained mysterious strain from Nelja N Maltseva, virologist who worked for more than 30 years at Research Institute for Viral Preparations in Moscow before her death two years ago; photo (M)

2002-12-03.title: New Athens Mayor Embodies a New Greece
2002-12-03.abstract: Dora Bakoyanni, first woman to be elected mayor of Athens, Greece, is one of most popular politicians in country with dismal record of representation by women in government; her dramatic life story has repeatedly intersected with darker chapters of Greece's recent past; her father, Constantine Mitsotakis, was prominent politician who was arrested in 1967 when military junta seized control of country; he then fled country with his family, returning in 1973 to be arrested again; he was released next year when junta fell; her husband, Pavlos Bakoyanni, an opponent of junta, was gunned down in 1989 by Nov 17 Group, which aimed its attacks at prominent public officials in Greece in effort to destabilize country; photo (M)

2002-12-03.title: A Mexican Daily's Downfall
2002-12-03.abstract: Excelsior, once prominent newspaper of Mexico, is bought by group of unidentified American and Canadian investors for $150 million; 25 years ago rulers of Institutional Revolutionary Party secretly bought newspaper's loyalty and over time drained it of independence; when Pres Vicente Fox's election ended Institutional Revolutionary Party's control, under-the-table subsidies and payoffs ended, and newspaper quickly fell into near bankruptcy (M)

2002-12-04.title: TURKEY SAYING NO TO ACCEPTING G.I.'S IN LARGE NUMBERS
2002-12-04.abstract: Turkey says US may not deploy substantial numbers of ground troops on its territory in event of war with Iraq; new government, led by party with Islamist roots, says US may station warplanes and use Turkish air space to carry out air strikes, but only if UN Security Council adopts new resolution authorizing use of force; Min Yasar Yakis cites Turkish public opinion in presenting position to Deputy Sec Paul D Wolfowitz; photo; map; Turks do not preclude stationing of Special Operations force and small ground units (M)

2002-12-04.title: World Briefing: The Americas
2002-12-04.abstract: HAITI: Protests Are Stopped; MEXICO: Toll Rises In Bus Crash;.

2002-12-04.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iran: Pollsters Go On Trial
2002-12-04.abstract: Trial begins for leading Iranian reformist politician and pollster Akbar Abdi along with two other pollsters; numerous charges against them include manipulation of public opinion polls (S)

2002-12-04.title: Bush to Press Turkish Leader for Military Support on Iraq
2002-12-04.abstract: President Bush has invited Recep Tayyip Erdogan to Washington in an effort to win broader support from Ankara for a possible military campaign in Iraq.

2002-12-04.title: British Plan Would Spare Some Fox Hunting
2002-12-04.abstract: British government offers compromise proposal that would end annual divisive debate by banning fox hunting in some areas and licensing it in places where it could be justified as pest control; stag and hare hunting would be banned altogether (S)

2002-12-05.title: Dutch Shut IKEA Stores After Bombs Are Found
Iraq Denounces Visit to Palace By U.N. Team
2002-12-05.abstract: Iraqi liaison with United Nations arms inspectors, Maj Gen Hussam Muhammad Amin, launches furious broadside against inspectors for their no-notice search of one of Pres Saddam Hussein's innumerable palaces and ascribes it to pressures from United States, Britain and Israel; holds news conference to again deny that Iraq has any banned nuclear, chemical or biological arms; day's events suggest conflicting pressures building in Baghdad, 72 hours before Iraq promises to deliver declaration on any secret weapons programs; Saddam Hussein will seek to meet UN's demands in ways that avoid war while saving his prestige, if not his weapons programs; Amin suggests that inspectors' palace visit was intended to humiliate Hussein; Iraq Foreign Ministry questions why inspectors did not wear protective suits for palace visit if they suspected there might be biological, chemical or nuclear weapons there (M)

2002-12-05.title: Chávez's Foes Plan to Extend General Strike In Venezuela
2002-12-05.abstract: Venezuelan businessmen and workers opposed to Pres Hugo Chavez's government extend paralyzing general strike into fourth day, possibly damaging informal accord made earlier in day between government and its foes in talks mediated by Organization of American States; strike threatens to cripple economy and disrupt oil supplies to United States; government had agreed in talks not to block nonbinding Feb 2 referendum on whether Chavez should stay in office and signaled that it would be open to talks on holding early presidential elections (M)

2002-12-05.title: Powell Says U.S. Will Increase Military Aid for Colombia
2002-12-05.abstract: Sec Colin L Powell says United States will increase military aid to Colombia to over $500 million a year, holding its war on leftist guerrillas and rightist paramilitary groups, and on their drug trafficking, is part of Bush administration's drive against terrorism; aid will be used for drug eradication, support for military and police forces and renewed support for Colombian narcotics-interception flights that rely on intelligence from American spy planes; Powell meets with Pres Alvaro Uribe Velez, Bogota; holds terrorism and drugs are linked in Colombia as threats to democracy; photo (M)

2002-12-05.title: Indonesia Holds Suspect In Bombings At Bali Clubs
2002-12-05.abstract: Indonesian police arrest Ali Ghufron, better known as Mukhlas, who is suspected of being senior commander in regional terrorist network Jemaah Islamiyah and key participant in Bali attack; diplomats and security officials hail arrest, in central Java, as big step in learning more about network and its links to Al Qaeda; he is brother of Amrozi, one of first people arrested in connection with Oct nightclub bombing; is also wanted in Singapore in Dec 2001 plot to blow up American Embassy there; arrest marks toughening in Indonesian government's attitude and policy toward terrorism; fact that he had not fled Indonesia raises questions about whereabouts of other Qaeda members and whether their list of potential hiding places is dwindling; United States and Australia are now focusing search for terrorists in area that includes southern Philippines and remote reaches of Borneo, island divided between Indonesia and Malaysia (M)

2002-12-06.title: Caftans and hothouse glamour:Mideast couture heats up
2002-12-06.abstract: PARIS—  As the world's purveyors of luxury goods huddle over designer-bag strategies in hopes that world turmoil will be averted and customers won't stop shopping, fashion in the Middle East is alive and kicking.

2002-12-06.title: Piura Journal; At 14, the Girl's Indomitable (Just Ask Her Father)
2002-12-06.abstract: Pres Alejandro Toledo of Peru, who was forced in October to acknowledge 14-year-old Zarai Toledo as his daughter after 13 years of court battles and increasingly public denials, now declares that he and Zarai are opening new chapter in their lives and that he has won 'an intelligent and beautiful daughter'; photos (M)

2002-12-06.title: Chechen Fugitive on His Way to Britain
2002-12-06.abstract: Akhmed Zakayev, senior Chechen representative at center of angry extradition dispute between Russian and Danish officials, leaves Denmark for Britain, raising prospect of similar dispute with British authorities (S)

2002-12-06.title: Russians Become Litigious: Survivors of Theater Siege Sue
2002-12-06.abstract: Moscow city government is being sued by five survivors of Chechen guerrilla seige of theater there in Oct and relatives of three who died; they seek compensation for physical and pschological damage they say resulted from their terrifying captivity and deadly raid by Russian police that ended it; sums plaintiffs are seeking are staggeringly high, equivalent of nearly $1 million each in city where average income is about $4,550; Moscow Mayor Yuri M Luzhkov says through spokesman that paying compensation would be equal to admitting guilt for terrorist act; says city will never do that; plaintiffs have won first victory when judge agrees to consider their claims; photos (M)

2002-12-06.title: Venezuelan Showdown: Chávez in Peril
2002-12-06.abstract: Embattled Venezuelan Pres Hugo Chavez is facing a more determined and single-minded opposition than in past, as nation enters fourth day of general strike; opposition is now contained in umbrella group known as Democratic Coordinator, and it has one objective: prodding Chavez into accepting nonbinding referendum on his rule on Feb 2 (M)

2002-12-07.title: Iraq Arms Quest Uncovers a Zest for Drink
2002-12-07.abstract: Customers at Tiger Eye liquor store in Baghdad react jokingly to visit by United Nations weapons inspectors to three gin factories outside city in search of nuclear bombs; drinking alcohol in public has been banned since government's 1996 faith campaign, bars and pubs have been closed, Iraqis now drink at home or furtively in restaurants and nightclubs but liquor shop business is booming; photos (M)

2002-12-07.title: Kyrgyzstan And Russia Sign New Pact
2002-12-07.abstract: Russian Pres Vladimir Putin, visiting Bishkek as part of Asian tour, signs new security pact with Kyrgyzstan; Russian air squadron and up to 1,000 Russian troops will be based by end of year in Kyrgyzstan as part of new rapid-reaction force (M)

2002-12-07.title: G.I.'s Walk Perilous Line Between Finding Enemy and Alienating Afghans
2002-12-07.abstract: Scene in Asadabad, Afghanistan, described as American troops walk fine line between searching for remnants of Taliban and Al Qaeda and not alienating fiercely independent Afghans; recent attacks and dozens of interviews with Afghans in Paktia, Khost and Kunar Provinces indicate enemy forces are growing bolder; photo; map (M)

2002-12-07.title: Berlusconi Tries New Field: Tin Pan Alley
2002-12-07.abstract: Italian Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi, former cruise ship crooner, reportedly has written lyrics for 18 love songs that will soon be released on CD; spokesman Paolo Bonaiuti says proceeds will go to Unicef; photo (M)

2002-12-07.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iran: U.S. Journalists To Be Fingerprinted
2002-12-07.abstract: Iranian government, responding to American policy of fingerprinting and photographing Iranians entering US, asks airport security to fingerprint and thoroughly document American journalists entering country (S)

2002-12-08.title: American Policies and Presence Are Under Fire in South Korea, Straining an Alliance
2002-12-08.abstract: Anti-Americanism is on rise in South Korea, fed by country's rapid passage to affluence and its perception that US is heavy handed and insensitive, particularly with regard to North Korea; Roh Moo Hyun, who holds narrow lead in presidential election, has repeatedly criticized US policies on Korea as 'hard-line' and promises that if elected he will guarantee security of North Korea; photo (M)

2002-12-08.title: Europeans Fear That the Threat From Radical Islamists Is Increasing
2002-12-08.abstract: Political leaders and police investigators across Europe conclude that threat of terrorism from Al Qaeda and other radical Islamic groups is more serious than they have assumed and may take years to neutralize; say recent investigations have uncovered surprisingly well-established networks of Muslim militants with potential to commit terrorist acts and affiliations that stretch across Europe to operatives in North America, North Africa, Middle East and Central Asia; disagree over extent to which Europe is actually more vulnerable to terrorism since Sept 11; map shows recent law enforcement activity against suspected terrorists in Europe (M)

2002-12-08.title: World Pageant Goes Ahead Over Protests; A Turk Wins
2002-12-08.abstract: Miss Turkey, Azra Akin, is crowned Miss World in London, where contest relocated from Nigeria after religious riots in connection with pageant left more than 200 people dead; show pauses to pay tribute to Amina Lawal, Nigerian woman sentenced to death by stoning by Islamic court for adultery (M)

2002-12-08.title: A Signal Moment Ahead: Will It Slow March to War?
2002-12-08.abstract: Patrick E Tyler analysis of whether declaration of weapons data by Iraq and ensuing UN inspection programs can forestall American invasion to unseat Saddam Hussein (M)

2002-12-08.title: Growing Poverty Is Shrinking Mexico's Rain Forest
2002-12-08.abstract: Mexico's remaining rain forests are disappearing as peasants slash and burn jungle to create little patches of land on which to farm; crops will soon fail because of thin topsoil, and then land will be grazed by cattle until rains wash earth away; Zapatista rebel movement is starting to turn against ecologists who want to save remains of forest, accusing them of waging 'war of extermination' against indigenous communities; photo; map (M)

2002-12-09.title: Chechen in Extradition Dispute: Criminal or Peacemaker?
2002-12-09.abstract: Russian government sees Akhmed Zakayev as Chechen terrorist and murderer, but others, even some in Russia, see him as best hope for peaceful resolution of long, bloody Chechen conflict; he is chief envoy of Chechnya's ousted president Aslan Maskhadov, and is now subject of Russia's second extradition dispute in recent weeks, this time with Britain; he was arrested on his arrival in London after authorities in Denmark rebuffed Russian demands to extradite him; he is now free on bail pending hearing; his fate has become test of Pres Vladimir V Putin's efforts to portray Chechen conflict as part of larger struggle against terrorism; Zakayev insists Chechnya's rebels want peace, but warns that some are prepared to stage new attacks like 57-hour siege of Moscow theater in Oct that left 129 hostages and 41 Chechen guerrillas dead; he has denounced that attack; photos (M)

2002-12-09.title: A Tough Case: How to Convict Hussein
2002-12-09.abstract: News analysis of difficulty of verifying suspicions about Iraq's weapons of mass destruction (M)

2002-12-09.title: A Top Iraqi Aide Defies U.S. to Find Proof of Weapons
2002-12-09.abstract: A general on Sunday denied that Iraq still has any weapons of mass destruction but hinted that, a decade ago, Baghdad came close to building an atom bomb.

2002-12-09.title: U.S. and Allies Must Increase Aid to Balkans, A Panel Asserts
2002-12-09.abstract: Report by Council on Foreign Relations warns that Balkan region remains tinderbox for ethnic tensions and criminality and calls for more forceful role for US, European Union and aid organizations; suggests European Union use carrot of admission to encourage sweeping reforms to reduce corruption, attract investment and nurture democracy (M)

2002-12-09.title: Israeli Army Kills Gaza Woman And Wounds Her 3 Children
2002-12-09.abstract: Palestinian woman is killed and her three children wounded by Israeli soldiers near Israeli settlement in Gaza; soldiers fired after spotting six armed Palestinian men approaching settlement; two soldiers are injured by bomb under jeep near Lebanon border; map (S)

2002-12-10.title: U.S. Says Iraqi Indicated Atom Project Is Continuing
2002-12-10.abstract: Bush administration seizes on Iraqi Gen Amir al-Saadi's admission that Iraq had sought to build nuclear weapon to argue that Pres Saddam Hussein has not give up quest; concedes it is reluctant to share with United Nations some American-gathered intelligence about Iraqi arms programs; pledges to review Iraqi declarations about Iraq's arms in thoughtful and thorough way (M)

2002-12-10.title: Pune Journal; Old Rajneesh Commune Lightens Up in Afterlife
2002-12-10.abstract: Followers of Osho, better known to Americans as Bhagwan Shree Rajneesh, late Indian self-help guru who set up commune in Antelope, Oregon, in 1970's, are still attracted to his commune, now more of a meditation resort, in Pune, India; power struggle has swirled among his devotees ever since he died of mysterious illness in 1990 at age 58; photos; map (M)

2002-12-10.title: Saburo Ienaga Is Dead at 89; Renegade Historian of Japan
Indonesia And Rebels Sign Accord; Outlook Murky
2002-12-10.abstract: Indonesia and Aceh Province separatists sign peace treaty, Geneva, in bid to end 26-year war; both sides warn accord will be hard to carry out; accord has slightly better chance of success than past peace efforts because it is strongly backed by international aid donors; calls for regional autonomy, local control over natural resources and elections for local legislature; makes no clear provisions for disarmament or demilitarization; map (M)

2002-12-10.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Regional Talks Delayed
2002-12-10.abstract: Meeting of South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation to be held in Islamabad, Pakistan, is postponed; India will not attend until Islamic militants are stopped from crossing Pakistan border into Kashmir (S)

2002-12-11.title: Brazilian Meets Bush and Vows to Cooperate on Freer Trade
2002-12-11.abstract: Brazilian Pres-elect Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, who campaigned against global reach of American power, meets with Pres Bush, Washington, pledging to work with him to open both nations' markets to freer trade; aides say they established working rapport despite their broad ideological differences; da Silva lauds Bush and says he can count on Bush as ally (M)

2002-12-11.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-12-11.abstract: SOUTH KOREA: Another Apology From U.S.; BANGLADESH: Opposition Cancels Strike; CHINA: Science Nods To Dinosaur Fiction;.

2002-12-11.title: Bribery Inquiry Involves Kazakh Chief, and He's Unhappy
2002-12-11.abstract: Kazakhstan quietly presses to exempt Pres Nursultan Nazarbayev from Justice Department probe of American oil companies that allegedly gave him large bribes to win oil concessions in 1990's; Bush administration officials have rebuffed effort, despite attempts by Nazarbayev and his representatives to raise issue with Vice Pres Dick Cheney and other senior officials; companies have denied wrongdoing, insisting all payments were made to Kazakh government; map; photos (M)

2002-12-11.title: 'As a Citizen of a Troubled World'
2002-12-11.abstract: Excerpts from Nobel Lecture delivered by ex-Pres Jimmy Carter (M)

2002-12-11.title: London Journal; In a Harsh Spotlight: the Very Private Cherie Blair
2002-12-11.abstract: london journal: in a harsh spotlight: the very

2002-12-12.title: IHT Insight: Low birthrates pose challenge for Europe
2002-12-12.abstract: COTTBUS, Germany:—  No one is entirely sure why so many women in this quaint, cobblestoned city south of Berlin stopped having babies 10 years ago. But it does not take a degree in sociology to see the effects of the rock-bottom birthrate: Thousands of schoolteachers have been laid off for lack of students. Workers have begun to dismantle, piece by piece, dozens of vacant apartments, part of a plan to demolish 5,000 units over the next decade. And the municipal water system is in need of a costly overhaul because water use is decreasing and stagnant water in pipes is a health hazard.

2002-12-12.title: World Business Briefing | Europe: Britain: Chechen Leader In Court
2002-12-12.abstract: Chechen envoy Akhmed Zakayev, wanted in Russia for murder and kidnapping, appears in London court for extradition hearing; was previously held in Demark for possible extradition, but was released (S)

2002-12-12.title: 70 U.N. Arms Monitors Extend Scope of Searches Into the Iraqi Desert
2002-12-12.abstract: Teams of United Nations weapons inspectors begin their third week of surprise visits to Iraqi industrial and scientific sites in mood of growing confidence that they can counter doubts voiced by senior Bush administration officials about their work; photo (M)

2002-12-12.title: A Secular India, or Not? At Strife Scene, Vote Is Test
2002-12-12.abstract: Dec 12 elections in India are seen latest test for many Indians of whether India's future will be as secular society, in which its 130 million Muslims have equal rights and protections, or communal one, in which they live at odds with, and perhaps at mercy of, its 820 million Hindus; no one is more concerned about outcome than Gujarat's Muslims, who feel their livelihoods and security may depend on results; anti-Muslim violence in Gujarat in February was led by Hindu nationalist groups closely linked to Bharatiya Janata Party, which governs Gujarat and leads national coalition as well; photos; map (M)

2002-12-12.title: World Business Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Migrants Being Deported
2002-12-12.abstract: Mexico announces deportation of 319 illegal migrants trying to reach United States; majority are from Ecuador; they had been traveling on two separate boats stoppped near Chiapas, Mexico (S)

2002-12-13.title: Iraq Opposition Is Pursuing Ties With Iranians
2002-12-13.abstract: American-backed Iraqi opposition is solidifying ties to Iran and opposing posibility of US-installed government if Saddam Hussein is ousted; leaders of all major groups, preparing to meet in London, welcome help getting rid of Hussein, but warn Iraq would not tolerate American military occupation or American 'viceroy' afterwards; reject US recommendation to refrain from setting up provisional government or assembly; Prof Kanan Makiya explains (M)

2002-12-13.title: Citizenship Delayed for 1,500; Security Check Backlog Cited
2002-12-13.abstract: Citizenship ceremonies for more than 1,500 immigrants in New York City are canceled, and federal officials say they cannot keep up with national demand for background checks on applicants (M)

2002-12-13.title: Iraq Cancels Oil Contract With 3 Russian Companies
2002-12-13.abstract: Iraq abruptly cancels contract with Russia's largest oil company, Lukoil, and two other Russian companies to develop major Iraqi oil field; Iraq's decision amounts to rebuff to Russia, one of Baghdad's strongest supporters at time when United States is threatening to go to war over what Bush administration says is Pres Saddam Hussein's pursuit of nuclear, biological and chemical weapons (M)

2002-12-13.title: Britain to Make Parents Pay If Their Children Play Hooky
2002-12-13.abstract: British government, under new plan intended to address problem of school absenteeism, proposes granting principals, welfare caseworkers and police officers right to fine parents of students who are chronically absent from school (S)

2002-12-13.title: After Banning 1,092 Movies, Chile Relaxes Its Censorship
2002-12-13.abstract: Chile, after enduring ridicule for its attitudes and being chastised by human rights orgns for suppressing free expression, is finally ending censorship system that has kept some of world's most popular and admired movies from being shown publicly; among films it has been illegal to exhibit or view for nearly 30 years have been Bernardo Bertolucci's Last Tango in Paris, Woody Allen's Everything You Ever Wanted to Know About Sex and Ingmar Bergman's Smiles of a Summer Night; Chile is probably most socially conservative nation in Latin America, in large part because Roman Catholic Church is even more powerful there than in neighboring countries (M)

2002-12-14.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Proposal To Recognize Sex Changes
2002-12-14.abstract: British government proposes legislation granting transsexuals right to be legally recognized, to marry under their chosen sex, to be granted birth certificates noting their new sex and to claim pensions at age 60 for women and 65 for men (S)

2002-12-14.title: Blair Rails at Reporters Over the Scandal That Won't Go Away
2002-12-14.abstract: British Prime Min Tony Blair, attending European Summit, tells reporters to 'move on' from obsession with his wife Cherie, her lifestyle adviser Carole Chaplin and Caplin's unsavory boyfriend, Peter Foster; Foster has served jail terms in connection with bogus diet-pill schemes, helped Blair's wife buy two apartments in Bristol and is fighting extradition to Australia; opinion polls show incident is beginning to inflict political damage (M)

2002-12-14.title: Kenya Clears Fishermen, Saying They Had No Ties to Attackers
2002-12-14.abstract: Kenyan Chief Magistrate Hanna Okwengu finds nine Somali and Pakistani fishermen detained in November hotel bombing and failed missile attack on airliner in Mombasa guilty of entering country illegally; orders them to pay fines or serve year in jail after which they would be deported; Deputy Police Comr William Langat says he no longer believes men had any links to attackers; another judge orders release on bail of mother and brother of Saleh Ali Saleh Nabhan, Kenyan who police say purchased vehicle used in hotel bombing; photo (M)

2002-12-14.title: Hussein Foes Meet in London, But Rivalries Fracture Unity
2002-12-14.abstract: Iraqi Opposition opens London Conference; delegates begin meeting and seek to draw political blueprint for post-Saddam Hussein Iraq; faction of delegates holds news conference in apparent attempt to set agenda without approval of its rivals; US has counseled convention organizers to limit agenda to show of unity and support for a democratic Iraq; photo (M)

2002-12-14.title: Australia Is Noncommittal On Scope of Any Role in Iraq
2002-12-14.abstract: US Deputy Secretary of State Richard L Armitage, visiting Australia during Asian tour, says America expects Australia to contribute to any US military effort against Iraq; statements by Prime Min John Howard and Foreign Min Alexander Downer indicate Australia, given deadly terrorist attack on Bali in October, would be contributing less to any Iraqi campaign than envisioned a few months ago (M)

2002-12-15.title: Kurdish Group Formed to Fight Secular Rule
Meeting of Iraqi Opposition Seeks to Bar U.S. Dominance
2002-12-15.abstract: Opponents of Iraqi Pres Saddam Hussein open conference in London in hopes of forming united front to plan for transitional government that would prevent United States from imposing its own vision on post-Hussein Iraq; photo (M)

2002-12-15.title: German and Spanish Navies Take On Major Role Near Horn of Africa
2002-12-15.abstract: German and Spanish navies take part in joint exercise in waters near Horn of Africa; are part of European antiterrorism naval task force established to monitor region; photos (M)

2002-12-15.title: Chávez Rejects Call by U.S. for Early Vote in Venezuela
2002-12-15.abstract: Venezuela Pres Hugo Chavez's government, whipsawed by 13-day strike that is debilitating economy, rejects Bush administration call for early elections to resolve political turmoil in oil-rich nation; government officials say that early election, as demanded by opposition, would be in violation of country's Constitution, which permits binding referendum on president's rule no earlier than August; photo (M)

2002-12-15.title: Hopes and Fears in India Stirred by Hindu Nationalist
2002-12-15.abstract: State assembly election in India's Gujarat State is viewed as referendum on Narendra Modi, who has ridden wave of Hindu nationalism since he was appointed chief minister by Bhartiya Janata Party just over year ago; critics say he is avatar of intolerance whose rise has coincided with increasing aggressiveness by Hindu extremists; supporters say he is hero who allowed Hindus to give fitting rejoinder to Muslims last spring, when 1,000 people, most of them Muslims, died in Hindu-Muslim riots; if Bharatiya Janata Party wins election, many fear Hindu extremists may export communal polarization; if it loses, coalition government it leads could be fatally weakened; photo; map (M)

2002-12-16.title: Former Chechen Rebel Leader, Once a Thorn in Russia's Side, Dies in Prison
2002-12-16.abstract: Russian officials report former Chechen guerrilla leader Salman Raduyeve, 35, has died of natural causes in Russian prison; he was captured in March 2000; was known for seizing over 2,000 hostages in raid during first Chechen war, humiliating Russian Army; photo (M)

2002-12-16.title: Venezuelan Leader Says He'll Weather Strike by Opponents
2002-12-16.abstract: Pres Hugo Chavez of Venezuela says he would consider resigning if Venezuela becomes ungovernable, but claims his government is bringing crisis under control; two weeks of broad-based national strikes aimed at ousting him have closed banks, stores and factories, and nearly shuttered oil industry that provides 14 percent of petroleum that United States imports; waves of generally peaceful demonstrations against Chavez are drawing hundreds of thousands of people to streets of Caracas; he claims continued support among poor and vows to outlast opponents, who vow to maintain strike until he agrees to early elections and resigns; he has rejected such calls; opponents describe him as authoritarian ruler who is taking Venezuela on path to economic ruin; photos (M)

2002-12-16.title: Canberra Journal; Australia Revisits a 'Black and White' Murder Case
2002-12-16.abstract: New film and reissue of book by historian Ken Inglis recall celebrated 1958 Australian murder case in which Aboriginal Rupert Max Stuart was sentenced to death for rape and murder of nine-year-old white girl; Stuart's sentence was commuted, partly because of efforts of Catholic priest and of Rohan Rivett, editor of Rupert Murdoch-owned newspaper, who began crusade for Stuart and persuaded Murdoch to take up cause; Inglis says Aboriginals now treat Stuart like royalty; map; photo (M)

2002-12-16.title: Blair Invites Palestinian Leaders to London
2002-12-16.abstract: The meeting in London is aimed at supporting reforms to the Palestinian Authority and advancing the Middle East peace process.

2002-12-16.title: U.S. Says Russia Helped Iran in Nuclear Arms Effort, Adding to Concerns About Allies
2002-12-16.abstract: American intelligence and defense officials believe Russia, key ally in anti-terrorism campaign, supplied Iran with much of equipment and expertise used to build two new facilities that seem to be part of nuclear arms program; Iran has denied it pursues nuclear arms and Russia insists all help has been for energy-related program (M)

2002-12-17.title: World Briefing | Asia: Hong Kong: Officials React To Protest
2002-12-17.abstract: Hong Kong officials estimate 60,000 people took part in march protesting new antisedition legislation; march prompted leaders to give consideration to popular view (S)

2002-12-17.title: Israelis Kill 3 Men in Gaza
2002-12-17.abstract: Israeli soldiers in Gaza Strip shoot and kill three Palestinians; two of dead are later found to have been carrying large bomb and other weapons (S)

2002-12-17.title: Transit Strikes Spread in Italy and Germany
2002-12-17.abstract: Nearly 120,000 workers seeking wage increase stage 24-hour strike that halts trains, buses and streetcars around Italy, joining string of job actions that have made holiday season season of strikes; Germany's streetcar and subway drivers also go on strike, refusing to work until government considers granting 3 percent wage increase (S)

2002-12-17.title: In His Politically Charged Trial, Russian Colonel Is Called Insane
2002-12-17.abstract: Two forensic psychiatrists testify that Russian Army colonel, Yuri Budanov, was insane when he strangled 18-year-old Chechen woman nearly three years ago; judgment of insanity--second such opinion in months--appears to pave way for Budanov's acquittal in court proceeding that has become yardstick for Russia's ability or willingess to control rights abuses by its military (S)

2002-12-17.title: Peace Accord Signed in Congo
2002-12-17.abstract: The power-sharing agreement aims to end the war that has ravaged Congo and roiled the continent for four years.

2002-12-18.title: Democracy and the War on Terrorism
2002-12-18.abstract: During the war on terrorism, George W. Bush has shown a split personality on the promotion of democracy abroad. Bush the realist seeks warm ties with dictators who may help in the fight against al Qaeda, while Bush the neo-Reaganite proclaims that democracy is the only true solution to terror. How the administration resolves this tension will define the future of U.S. foreign policy.

2002-12-18.title: In Grief, Israeli Family Questions Army Aid to Settlers
2002-12-18.abstract: Family of Cpl Keren Yaacoby, first Israeli woman in army to die in combat since Sept 2000, questions high cost to other Israelis of protecting religious Jewish settlements in West Bank; Yaacoby was killed with fellow soldier by Palestinian gunmen while guarding Hebron settlement; settlers, paying visit to mourners, defend settlements; photos (M)

2002-12-18.title: Iraq and the Arabs' Future
2002-12-18.abstract: The driving motivation behind a new U.S. endeavor in Iraq should be modernizing the Arab world. Most Arabs will see such an expedition as an imperial reach into their world. But in this case a reforming foreign power's guidelines offer a better way than the region's age-old prohibitions, defects, and phobias. No apologies ought to be made for America's "unilateralism."

2002-12-18.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Mad Cow Treatment
2002-12-18.abstract: British High Court will allow experimental drug to be given to two teenagers dying of Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease, human form of mad cow disease; British government has purchased Life Resources, Inc, out of New York in move to ensure untainted blood supply for National Health Service (S)

2002-12-18.title: U.S. Ignores Failure Data At Outset Of Flights
2002-12-18.abstract: Pentagon's new assessment process for prototype antimissile system ignores failures in earlier, less challenging stages, focusing only on interceptor warheads that make it to phase where they try to find, home in on and hit mock warheads; did not count as failure last week's $100 million test in which interceptor did not separate from booster rocket and thus missed intended target by hundreds of miles; private experts say new logic cleared way for Pres Bush's decision to deploy interceptors in Alaska and California; critics say counting all tests show antimissile defense is mirage; photo (M)

2002-12-19.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Top General In Chechnya Is Fired
2002-12-19.abstract: Russian Pres Vladimir V Putin fires Chechnya commander Colonel General Gennadi N Troshev after his public refusal to accept Defense Ministry transfer to command in Siberia; war in Chechnya has frustrated Defense Minister Sergei B Ivanov's attempts at Russian military reforms (S)

2002-12-19.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Rights Talks End Promisingly
2002-12-19.abstract: Two-day talks between US officials, headed by Assistant Secretary of State Lorne Craner, and Chinese diplomats, end with China's promise to give blanket access to United Nations human rights monitors (S)

2002-12-19.title: Liberal Wins South Korea's Presidential Election
2002-12-19.abstract: Roh Moo Hyun's campaign urged continued engagement with North Korea and greater autonomy from the U.S.

2002-12-19.title: World Briefing | Europe: France: Trying To Cut Traffic Deaths
2002-12-19.abstract: French government proposes variety of regulations attempting to reduce highway deaths; France has fourth highest fatal accident rate in Europe; sanctions include new speed-detection systems, higher fines and stricter licensing laws; zero tolerance for people who drink and drive is also being considered, but not without controversy (S)

2002-12-19.title: Full Text: In Blix's Words
2002-12-19.abstract: Notes for briefing the Security Council regarding inspections in Iraq and a preliminary assessment of Iraq's declaration under paragraph 3 of resolution 1441

2002-12-20.title: World Briefing | Europe: Northern Ireland: Catholics Increase
2002-12-20.abstract: Northern Ireland census figures show small increase in Catholic population to 44 percent from 42 percent ten years ago; Catholics, however, are now majority in four out of five cities (S)

2002-12-20.title: World Briefing | Asia: Myanmar: Backers Of Opposition Harassed
2002-12-20.abstract: Myanmar's pro-democracy leader Aung San Suu Kyi, on her first trip since house arrest ended, is met with silence rather than exuberance on her trip to Maruk Oo; supporters hold usual crowds were disbanded by military; 20,000 people are ordered to disassemble next day in lieu of rally (S)

2002-12-20.title: World Briefing | Asia: The Philippines: Brief Parole For Ex-President
2002-12-20.abstract: Former Philippine Pres Joseph Estrada is allowed short leave from military hospital detention to visit Mother Ignacia Healing Center to receive holy water treatments for his arthritic knees; Estrada's request for medical treatment in US was denied; is being held on charge of plunder (S)

2002-12-20.title: Iraq Insists U.S. Experts Are Rushing To Judgment
2002-12-20.abstract: Iraqi Gen Amir al-Saadi, apparently seeking to play down any criticism that emerged from United Nations chief arms inspector Hans Blix's report, holds politicians in United States and Britain are making false statements without giving scientists time to properly evaluate Iraq's lengthy declaration of its weapons programs; says Iraq awaits assessment by specialists, not politicians (M)

2002-12-20.title: Israeli Soldiers Evict 250 Settlers From a New West Bank Outpost
2002-12-20.abstract: Israeli soldiers in Hebron, West Bank, evict some 250 Jewish settlers from outpost erected in Nov near road to Cave of Patriarchs in response to Palestinian ambush that killed 12 Israeli soldiers; arrest six settlers; Palestinian girl, 11, is killed, apparently by stray bullet fired by Israeli soldiers during angry demonstration by mourners at funeral of youth killed earlier, Rafah, Gaza Strip (M)

2002-12-21.title: A Nation That Exports Oil, Herring and Peace
2002-12-21.abstract: Article on Norway's history as broker of peace; diplomats and government officials note country has no colonial past, has no grand designs on other countries, does not engender suspicion, has no serious alliances, has financed much humanitarian work, is remote and is relatively untroubled; photos (M)

2002-12-21.title: Declaration Lists Companies That Sold Chemicals to Iraq
2002-12-21.abstract: American officials and private weapons specialists say 12,000-page weapons declaration that Iraq delivered to UN on December 7 lists 31 major foreign companies that sold chemicals and equipment needed for Iraq's chemical weapons program before 1991; list reportedly includes Alcolac International of Maryland and Al Haddad trading company of Tennessee, both now defunct, 14 German companies, three each from Netherlands and Switzerland and two each from France and Austria; list, obtained by lawyers for ailing Gulf War veterans, could be important as veterans pursuit lawsuits against some companies over their health problems (M)

2002-12-21.title: World Briefing | Asia: Kashmir: Three Unveiled Women Are Killed
2002-12-21.abstract: Nosen Kousar, 20, and Shahnaaz Akhtqar, 21, are killed in their homes and third woman, Tahira Parveen, 22, is taken away and beheaded in Rajouri district of Kashmir; local officials say women may have been killed for not wearing veils (S)

2002-12-21.title: World Briefing | Europe: Vatican: Mother Teresa Nears Sainthood
2002-12-21.abstract: Pope John Paul II beatifies Mother Teresa, citing her posthumous role in curing Indian woman of cancer and calling her 'world emblem of Christian charity'; photo (S)

2002-12-21.title: Fans Think They'll Die; Some Might
2002-12-21.abstract: Scientists from University of Bristol and University of Birmingham, reporting in current issue of British Medical Journal, offer medical evidence supporting link between higher risk of heart attacks and penalty shootout during 1998 World Cup soccer match between Argentina and England (S)

2002-12-22.title: U.S. SAID TO READY KURD AREAS IN IRAQ FOR POSSIBLE WAR
2002-12-22.abstract: American preparations for war against Iraq are under way in independent Kurdish northern Iraq; American intelligence officials are working alongside Kurdish officials, and recruiters for American-sponsored opposition group are selecting candidates for program to train scouts and translators that one day may help American forces inside Iraq; potential basing sites for use in war are being studied; no American military forces are based there yet, and recent Turkish and Arabic news reports of sizable military deployment appear unfounded; photo; map (M)

2002-12-22.title: Editors' Note; Left Behind
2002-12-22.abstract: More on Pages 16-17:  The Industry  The Anger  The Women  The Raid

2002-12-22.title: Brazil's New Cabinet Offers Surprises, and Draws Criticism
2002-12-22.abstract: Some of people Pres-elect Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva of Brazil has selected to serve in his cabinet face charges of conflict of interest or opposition from within his own fractious coalition; these include Luiz Furlan, development and foreign trade minister-designate, Vice Pres-elect Jose Alencar and Roberto Rodrigues, agriculture minister-designate, Gilberto Gil, culture minister-designate, and Henrique Meirelles, who will be president of Central Bank; da Silva wins praise for his choice for environmental minister, Sen Marina Silva, rubber tapper's daughter and close associate of slain Amazon ecologist Chico Mendes (M)

2002-12-22.title: Consuls Lax In Screening For Visas, Report Says
2002-12-22.abstract: State Department inspector general reports that system for issuing nonimmigrant visas remains too lax and poorly financed to screen out determined terrorists; reports that many consular officials are inexperienced or inadequately trained, that visa review procedures vary from embassy to embassy and that program to identify terrorism suspects needs more money (M)

2002-12-22.title: 2 Afghan Detainee Deaths Investigated
2002-12-22.abstract: Two detainees have died in American military custody at the Bagram Air Base, north of Kabul, this month, prompting an army investigation.

2002-12-23.title: Reporter's Notebook; Some in Strike-Ravaged Venezuela Say Forget About Politics and Dance
2002-12-23.abstract: Reporter's Notebook from Venezuela, where national strike that has pushed country to brink of social chaos is in its 20th day; photos (M)

2002-12-23.title: Qaeda Suspect Was Taking Flight Training Last Month
2002-12-23.abstract: CIA reports that Abd al-Rahim al-Nashiri, reputed chief of operations for Al Qaeda in Persian Gulf region, was taking flight lessons near strategic shipping lanes in Strait of Hormuz when he was arrested by authorities of United Arab Emirates and turned over to agency; says interrogation of Nashiri has thus far yielded no information about Qaeda plans or clues to whereabouts of Osama bin Laden; photo (M)

2002-12-23.title: Afghanistan and Its Neighbors Sign a Pact to Keep the Peace
2002-12-23.abstract: Representatives of Afghanistan's six neighbors sign declaration of nonaggression; pact with Pakistan, China, Iran, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan is vitally important for Afghanistan, which in past two decades has suffered Soviet occupation and internal conflicts abetted by its neighbors, particularly Pakistan and Iran; Pres Hamid Karzai pledges that Afghanistan will never allow itself to be used for aggression against another country (M)

2002-12-23.title: For U.N. Labor of Hercules, A Talk- and Walk-Through
2002-12-23.abstract: United Nations inspectors visit Iraq's space research and development center in Baghdad, and slow, deliberate methods they employ underscore herculean task ahead of them; Gen Amir al-Saadi, Pres Saddam Hussein's chief weapons adviser, says Iraq has no further information to provide and challenges CIA officers to come and point out suspect sites to inspectors; photo (M)

2002-12-23.title: A Hong Kong Rally For Tough New Laws Pushed by Beijing
2002-12-23.abstract: Large crowd demonstrates in Hong Kong in favor local government's plan for strict internal security laws, including long prison terms for sedition and broad police discretion for searches without warrants in security cases (S)

2002-12-24.title: A Holy Night Prayer in Bethlehem: Send More Tourists
2002-12-24.abstract: The baby Jesus figurines are stacking up on a table at a workshop here, unsold and not even completely carved. Why bother? asked William Khano, who has been carving biblical figures from olive wood for 35 of his 47 years. There are few tourists to buy them anyway.    ''There is no Christmas,'' he said. ''There is no joy. There are no festivals, no celebrations. Only prayers.''

2002-12-24.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-12-24.abstract: ITALY: King'S Son Returns, Briefly, From Exile; KOSOVO: Albanians Arrested In Killing Of Serb; LITHUANIA: Presidential Run-Off; BRITAIN: King Tut'S Curse A Myth, Study Says; SWEDEN: Man Sentenced For Taking Gun On Plane;.

2002-12-24.title: Brushing Aside Objections, Hong Kong Proceeds on Security Law
2002-12-24.abstract: Brushing aside objections, the Hong Kong today ended a public comment period on plans for tough internal security laws and said it would proceed directly with legislation.

2002-12-24.title: North Korea Begins to Reopen Plant for Processing Plutonium
2002-12-24.abstract: North Korea has started to reopen sealed plutonium reprocessing plant at Yongbyon nuclear complex, most provocative and technically important step it has taken in recent days to revive its nuclear arms program; International Atomic Energy Agency says North Koreans have disabled surveillance cameras and broken through seals at pool holding 8,000 spent fuel rods; Bush administration stresses it will continue to deal with situation diplomatically; Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld warns United States is capable of confronting North Korea militarily, news conference; bipartisan pressure grows from Congress for Bush to rethink policy of not negotiating until North Korea drops nuclear program; photos; map (M)

2002-12-24.title: U.S. Public Is Unconvinced on Need to Wage War Against Iraq, Says Council on Foreign Relations President Les Gelb
2002-12-24.abstract: Council on Foreign Relations, December 2002 - Les Gelb, the president of the Council on Foreign Relations, says that he is surprised by the degree of opposition in the United States to an invasion of Iraq. On a recent speaking tour, Gelb says 80 to 90 percent of audience members were against an invasion, which he says is likely by March unless Saddam Hussein is first overthrown through a coup. To get the public on its side, Gelb said it was imperative for the Bush administration to provide "a smoking gun" - conclusive evidence - that Saddam has weapons of mass destruction. On other matters, Gelb, a former high State Department official in the Carter administration, says that he is skeptical that progress toward a Middle East peace can be achieved in the next year or two.

2002-12-25.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-12-25.abstract: PHILIPPINES: Bomb Kills 13; MALAYSIA: U.S. To Clear Visa Backlog; CAMBODIA: Talks On Tribunal; THAILAND: Condoms For Scouts;.

2002-12-25.title: Afghans Mark Year of Slowly Growing Stability
2002-12-25.abstract: Afghanistan has achieved degree of stability in year since Hamid Karzai began governing, even if it is not yet united country with functioning multiethnic army; Karzai acknowledges some failures of his government, namely security and disarmament, and vows to tackle those issues more energetically in year ahead; Burhanuddin Rabbani, former president who last year opposed Karzai's presidency, now voices unreserved support for him and central government; American forces based in country now contemplate moving from combat operations toward development activities, deploying larger teams of civil affairs officers in 8 to 10 provincial centers to give boost to economic development; American officials say battle against Taliban and Al Qaeda is drawing to end and effort to win peace and help government establish its control countrywide is new target; economic condition of country remain precarious; regional warlords continue to flex enormous power, but Karzai is intent on winning them over by persuasion rather than force of arms (M)

2002-12-25.title: Safety Chief Tries to Make Travel Easy, Too
2002-12-25.abstract: Interview with Adm James M Loy, undersecretary of transportation for security and head of Transportation Security Administration, which is scrambling to meet Dec 31 deadline for start of airport screening of all checked baggage; his can-do philosophy and lighthearted demeanor have won him praise from both parties on Capitol Hill and even within ranks of airlines and airport managers whose business he is restructuring; photo (M)

2002-12-25.title: Spain Loves a Pop Music Show, but Critics Hate the Reality
2002-12-25.abstract: Operacion Triunfo, extremely popular Spanish reality television show that manufactures pop stars, prompts quixotic campaign by more than 100 musicians and critics, who complain about show's near monopoly of television music; show's contestants dominate Spanish record charts, and critics say show is elbowing out other music programs from prime-time space on public television (M)

2002-12-25.title: BEIJING RELEASES LEADING DISSIDENT
2002-12-25.abstract: China releases Xu Wenli, its most prominent pro-democracy prisoners, sending him to exile and medical treatment in US; Wenli, who spent more than 16 of last 21 years in prison for his irrepressible advocacy of civil liberties, suffers from chronic and progressive infection with hepatitis B; his release signals China's strong desire for good relations with US; photo of Xu being greeted by his daughter at O'Hare Airport in Chicago (M)

2002-12-26.title: Poland Said to Pick Lockheed for $3.5 Billion Fighter Deal
2002-12-26.abstract: Poland appeared set to award a contract to the Lockheed Martin Corporation to build 48 advanced fighter planes for the Polish Air Force at a cost of $3.5 billion.

2002-12-26.title: U.S. Army Finds Allies In the U.S. Air Force
2002-12-26.abstract: Reporter's Notebook on Gen Richard B Myers's visit to US air base in Kuwait; AH-64 Apache gunship, Army's main attack helicopter, and Air Force's RQ-1 Predator, propeller-driven robot plane that looks like upside-down spoon, could form deadly new tandem in US-led war in Iraq; Myers visit focuses on morale of US forces in region (S)

2002-12-26.title: Persistent Drop in Fertility Reshapes Europe's Future
2002-12-26.abstract: Fertility rates in many European countries have plummeted over last decades, shifting one-child families close to statistical norm; World Health Orgn figures show Europe to be continent with lowest fertility rate; no West European country has total fertility rate of 2.1--marker that demographers say means exact replenishment of population; in United States rate is 2.0, which demographers attribute to greater immigration; trend in Europe has been evident for many years, but its slow-building consequences are now coming into starker relief; more West European countries are taking new steps to address specter of sharply winnowed and less competitive work forces, surfeits of retirees and pension systems that will need to be cut back deeply; fertility rates are starkest in East European countries, but trend hit Western Europe earlier, and has had more time to produce hand-wringing and soul-searching; among reasons for trend are that people are studying longer and marrying later, that contraception and abortion are readily available, that divorce is more common and that would-be parents' attachments to leisure time, conveniences and indulgences do not easily accommodate multiple children--or sometimes, any children at all; photo; graphs (L)

2002-12-26.title: Pope Calls for War Fires to Be Extinguished
2002-12-26.abstract: Pope John Paul II's Christmas message from St Peter's Square speaks out against prospect of war in Middle East and urges people not to succumb to fear of terrorism (M)

2002-12-26.title: Growing U.S. Need for Oil From the Mideast Is Forecast
2002-12-26.abstract: Energy Department predicts that Western dependence on Mideast oil will increase in two decades, even as Pres Bush seeks to reduce US reliance on such imports; forecast is that by 2025, 51 percent of world oil production will come from OPEC, which now produces 38 percent of world's oil; about two-thirds of OPEC production comes from Persian Gulf; graph (S)

2002-12-27.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-12-27.abstract: CYPRUS: Demand For Reunification; BRITAIN: A Final Tally-Ho?;.

2002-12-27.title: World Briefing | Asia: Japan: Illegal Immigrants Caught
2002-12-27.abstract: Japanese authorities, following tip from customs, arrest 52 illegal immigrants thought to have come from China; aliens hid on Bolivian-registered ship in freezer car later used in foiled getaway (S)

2002-12-27.title: Iraq to Identify Scientists
2002-12-27.abstract: Iraq said it would give the United Nations a list of hundreds of scientists who have worked on nuclear, chemical, biological and missile programs. Page A12.

2002-12-27.title: Allied Jets Bomb Military Site; Iraq Says Air Strike Killed 3
U.S. Says Evidence Confirms Reports of Mass Rapes by Burmese
2002-12-27.abstract: State Department reports obtaining evidence that mass rapes of hundreds of girls and women were carried out by Burmese army in Shan Province, Myanmar, where military government has tried for years to suppress ethnic rebellion; says United States has expressed 'deep concern' about rapes and other abuses to Myanmar and urged government to punish guilty; US has also called on United Nations to probe charges, first made by human rights groups (M)

2002-12-28.title: U.N. Inspectors Query Scientist, Who Denies Tie To Iraqi Arms
2002-12-28.abstract: UN nuclear weapons inspectors interview Iraqi scientist Kathim Jamil on his alleged involvement in early stage of clandestine nuclear program; Jamil calls Iraqi television program to dispel notion of involvement in nuclear program; says he is metallurgist working on restoring aluminum tubes (M)

2002-12-28.title: World Briefing: Middle East
2002-12-28.abstract: SAUDI ARABIA: Deadly Cologne, Down The Hatch; IRAN: Video Dealers Will Be Whipped; TURKEY: Prosecutor Seeks 1,235-Year Sentence;.

2002-12-28.title: World Briefing | Europe: Ukraine: Court Rejects Charges Against President
2002-12-28.abstract: Ukrainian Supreme Court rejects opposition effort to pursue corruption, abuse of power and arms sales charges against Pres Leonid Kuchma; photo (S)

2002-12-28.title: Navy Hospital Ship Is Activated for Duty
2002-12-28.abstract: In another major sign of preparations for a possible war against Iraq, the Navy has activated one of its two 1,000-bed hospital ships, the Comfort, to be sent for possible duty in the Persian Gulf.    Activating the Comfort signals that the Bush administration is preparing for large numbers of American casualties, and is ready to take the risk to back up its threat to use military force to disarm Iraq. The Comfort has 12 operating rooms and is equipped to deal with major trauma cases and casualties from chemical and biological weapons.

2002-12-28.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-12-28.abstract: KYRGYZSTAN: Explosion In Market Kills 7; MYANMAR: Accusations Of Mass Rape Denied;.

2002-12-29.title: German Minister Softens Stance Against Iraq War
2002-12-29.abstract: Foreign Min Joschka Fischer declines to rule out possibility of Germany voting for military action against Iraq in United Nations Security Council; comments reflect degree of flexibility absent in Chancellor Gerhard Schroder's earlier insistence that Germany would not support war even in event of affirmative vote in Security Council (S)

2002-12-29.title: Iraq Gives the U.N. a List Of 500 Weapons Experts
2002-12-29.abstract: Iraq hands over to United Nations office in Baghdad list of more than 500 experts in development of ballistice missiles and nuclear, chemical or biological weapons, fulfilling one requirement of United Nations Security Council Resolution 1411; extent to which scientists will prove helpful in ferreting out any new information about Iraq's possible weapons of mass destruction remain open question; Kadhim Mijbel, second Iraqi scientists questioned by nuclear inspectors, suggests at news conference that all Iraqi scientists should demand that witness from government be present at interviews with inspectors and that no one should leave country to be interviewed (M)

2002-12-29.title: South Koreans Divided on North Korean Atom Threat
2002-12-29.abstract: Many younger South Koreans are siding with North Korea in its confrontation with United States over its nuclear program and see their country as caught in middle of conflict; about 70 prostesters hold rally in Seoul against North Korea's nuclear program, but several thousands largely young people hold candlelight vigil outside US Embassy, demanding more equal partnership between Seoul and Washington and expulsion of 37,000 American troops stationed in South Korea; photo (M)

2002-12-29.title: A Restive Kosovo, Officially Still Serbian, Squirms Under the Status Quo
2002-12-29.abstract: Ethnic Albanians in Kosovo are growing increasingly impatient with being Serbian province; they and Serbs would both opt for partition -- trading northern parts of Kosovo where most Serbs live for land inhabited by Albanians in other parts of southern Serbia -- even though it could open Pandora's box of border disputes, potentially threatening territorial integrity of both Bosnia and Macedonia; international bureaucrats who administer province want to defer decision on its ultimate status, but they are also eager to end their involvement there; map; photo; chronology of conflict in Kosovo (M)

2002-12-29.title: Saudis Are Said To Assure U.S. On Use of Bases
2002-12-29.abstract: Senior US officials say Saudi Arabia has given them assurances that kingdom will make its airspace, air bases and important operations center at Prince Sultan Air Base outside Riyadh available to US in event of war with Iraq; are confident that Saudis will ultimately allow attack missions, which are more politically sensitive, to be flown from their soil; Saudis remain publicly noncommittal; uncertainty about Saudi cooperation led Pentagon to build alternative air command post in Qatar, where overall American command for Iraqi operations will be headquartered (M)

2002-12-30.title: 'Let's Look Out for Australia,' An Antiterror Campaign Urges
2002-12-30.abstract: Australian government starts advertising campaign urging people to be alert to terrorist dangers at home; campaign takes pains not to single out Australia's Muslim population, which numbers about 400,000, and to emphasize country's diversity (M)

2002-12-30.title: Venezuela Strikers Keep Pressure on Chávez and Oil Exports
2002-12-30.abstract: Hundreds of thousands of Venezuelans fill streets of Caracas to declare their commitment to national strike, now in its 28th day, to force ouster of Pres Hugo Chavez; strike, joined by 30,000 oil workers, threatens to wreak havoc on nation, world's fifth-largest oil producer; photo (M)

2002-12-30.title: Death Toll Rises in Bombing of Russian Offices in Chechnya
2002-12-30.abstract: As the unofficial death toll for last week's attack in Grozny rose, President Vladimir V. Putin warned that militants are employing more violent tactics.

2002-12-30.title: Kenya's Ruling Party Is Defeated After 39 Years
2002-12-30.abstract: Kenyans elect opposition leader Mwai Kibaki as their new president, in resounding defeat for party that has ruled over country for nearly four decades; result leaves no doubt about voters' opinion of 24-year rule of retiring president Daniel arap Moi, whose chosen successor Uhuru Kenyatta is rejected throughout country; this is first election governing party has lost since independence in 1963; election officials declare Kibaki winner as ballots are still being tabulated; say margin is so great that none of his four rivals stand chance; independent election watchers give Kibaki 63 percent of vote, compared with 20 percent for Kenyatta, second-place finisher; Kibaki is former finance minister and vice president to Moi who broke with him decade ago; has vowed to change Kenya's fortunes by turning tide on corruption, poverty and authoritarian rule; Moi's decision to retire, as required by country's Constitution, and to allow bitter rival to replace him is seen as milestone in development of Kenya's democracy; photos; map; brief recap of country's history since 1963 (L)

2002-12-30.title: U.S. Plan for Trade Penalties for North Korea Worries South Korean Investors
2002-12-30.abstract: Bush administration's plan to use trade sanctions to force North Korea to abandon its nuclear bomb program worries South Korean investors who do business in North Korea; South Korea is North's second-largest trading partner after China; photo; map (L)

2002-12-31.title: Israel Rules Reserves Must Serve in Occupied Territories
2002-12-31.abstract: Israeli Supreme Court rules that reserve soldiers cannot refuse to serve in occupied territories; ruling is setback to group of soldiers and officers who say they will serve elsewhere, but refuse to serve in West Bank or Gaza; group has 512 members but has not gained much political traction for its claim that occupation is corrupting Israeli society; group founder Lt David Zonshein is returned to military prison to serve remainder of sentence for refusing call-up; in latest violence, Israeli soldiers kill eight Palestinians in various incidents; photo (M)

2002-12-31.title: World Briefing | Asia: Philippines: Police Can't Shoot For Fun
2002-12-31.abstract: Philippine police are no longer allowed to fire their weapons in celebration of New Year's Eve in effort to reduce accidental shootings; guns will be monitored with taped muzzles and hotline established for public to report illegal police firings; there are no laws preventing general population from celebrating with gunfire (S)

2002-12-31.title: Irish Church to Cooperate With Abuse Inquiry
2002-12-31.abstract: Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Dublin pledges cooperation with any police investigation into sex abuse allegations against priests and says officers will have full access to church files; assurance comes after Cardinal Desmond Connell meets with abuse victims Ken Reilly and Marie Collins, who had demanded his resignation and threatened to stage protest (M)

2002-12-31.title: 3 U.S. Citizens Slain in Yemen In Rifle Attack
2002-12-31.abstract: Yemeni officials report three Americans killed at Baptist missionary hospital in central Yemen by man apparently belonging to small cell that plans to victimize both Westerners and secular Yemenis; fourth American is seriously wounded in attack; assailant, identified as Abed Abdul-Razak al-Kamil, sneaks semiautomatic rifle into hospital under his coat; Kamil surrenders to authorities and is arrested; this is latest in series of Islamic militant attacks against easily accessible American targets in region; officials in Washington say it is too early to know whether Kamil acted alone or is linked with Al Qaeda, whose leader Osama bin Laden has family origins in Yemen; guns are so common in Yemen that missionary hospital where attack occurred, in Jibla, has desk where visitors are supposed to check them; assailant easily evades site and bursts into room where staff meeting was taking place; those killed are hospital director William E Koehn, Dr Martha C Kyers and purchasing agent Kathleen A Gariety; pharmacist Donald W Caswell is shot but not killed; Kamil tells investigators he listened to speeches of Ali Mohammed Jarallah, member of Islamic Reform Party, who is being questioned by Yemeni authorities in connection with recent assassination of Socialist Party's deputy leader; Islamic militants are said to be resentful that missionary workers preach Christianity; photos; map (L)

2002-12-31.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Another Step Toward Mars?
2002-12-31.abstract: China launches fourth unmanned spacecraft in program aimed toward eventual exploration of moon and Mars; Shenzhou IV lifts off from Gobi Desert on seven-day mission (S)
